setTitle: AWS SAA Practice - IAM & EC2 Easy Set 1

questions.questionText: What does IAM stand for in AWS?

A.reason: A made-up term — AWS has no service by this name.

B.reason: The full name of the AWS IAM service, which manages users, groups, roles, and policies to control access to AWS resources.

C.reason: A generic software architecture term, not an AWS service.

D.reason: A fabricated term with no meaning in AWS.

questions.Tips: IAM = Identity and Access Management — the word 'Identity' is the clue. Eliminate options that use unrelated IT jargon (Internet, Integrated, Internal) that don't match AWS terminology.

questions.questionText: Which IAM entity is best suited for granting permissions to an AWS service (e.g., EC2) to access other AWS resources?

A.reason: A long-term identity for a person or application with permanent credentials.

B.reason: A container for organizing multiple IAM Users and assigning shared policies.

C.reason: An identity that provides temporary credentials and can be assumed by AWS services, users, or applications.

D.reason: A JSON document defining permissions. It cannot be directly attached to a service instance; it must be attached via a role or user.

questions.Tips: The question asks about granting permissions to an AWS *service*, not a human. Services cannot be added to Groups, and a Policy alone isn't an identity. Between User and Role, only Roles are designed to be *assumed* by services — eliminate User.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended best practice when the AWS root account is first created?

options.A: Use the root account for all daily administrative tasks
options.C: Enable MFA on the root account and avoid using it for everyday tasks
options.D: Share the root credentials with the security team

A.description: Use the root account for all daily administrative tasks
A.reason: Using root for everything exposes the most privileged account to unnecessary risk.

B.reason: The root account cannot be deleted; it is permanently tied to the AWS account.

C.description: Enable MFA on the root account and avoid using it for everyday tasks
C.reason: Enabling MFA adds a second factor of authentication, significantly reducing the risk of unauthorized access to the most powerful account in AWS.

D.description: Share the root credentials with the security team
D.reason: Sharing root credentials multiplies the attack surface and violates the principle of least privilege.

questions.Tips: Root account best practice = lock it down, not use it. Eliminate 'use it daily' (too risky) and 'share it' (always wrong). 'Delete' is impossible — AWS won't let you. That leaves MFA + minimal use.

questions.questionText: Which of the following EC2 instance purchasing options provides the highest discount compared to On-Demand pricing?

A.reason: A legacy option allowing reservation of capacity on a recurring schedule. Provides modest savings.

B.reason: Spare EC2 capacity offered at up to 90% discount compared to On-Demand. AWS can reclaim them with a 2-minute warning.

C.reason: Provides a ~20–40% discount in exchange for a 1-year commitment with no upfront payment.

D.reason: Pay-as-you-go with no commitment. This is the baseline price — no discount at all.

questions.Tips: Highest discount = most risk/restriction. On-Demand has zero discount. Reserved (1yr, no upfront) is moderate. Spot wins with up to 90% off because AWS can interrupt them — maximum savings, maximum risk.

questions.questionText: An EC2 instance needs to read objects from an S3 bucket. What is the most secure way to grant this permission?

options.A: Hardcode AWS credentials in the application code
options.B: Attach an IAM Role with the required S3 permissions to the EC2 instance
options.C: Create an IAM User and store its access key on the EC2 instance
options.D: Make the S3 bucket publicly accessible

A.description: Hardcode AWS credentials in the application code
A.reason: Embedding access keys directly in code creates a serious security risk, especially if the code is stored in version control.

B.description: Attach an IAM Role with the required S3 permissions to the EC2 instance
B.reason: IAM Roles provide temporary, automatically rotated credentials to the instance without the need to manage static keys.

C.description: Create an IAM User and store its access key on the EC2 instance
C.reason: Storing long-term access keys on an instance is risky — if the instance is compromised, the keys can be stolen.

D.description: Make the S3 bucket publicly accessible
D.reason: Opening the bucket to the public exposes all objects to anyone on the internet, not just the EC2 instance.

questions.Tips: Any option involving hardcoding, static keys, or public access is always wrong for security questions. IAM Role on EC2 is the only option with no long-term credentials stored anywhere.

questions.questionText: Which EC2 instance type family is optimized for compute-intensive workloads such as batch processing and scientific modeling?

A.reason: Burstable general-purpose instances designed for workloads with low baseline CPU usage that occasionally spike.

B.reason: Memory-optimized instances designed for workloads that require large amounts of RAM, such as in-memory databases.

C.reason: Compute-optimized instances with a high ratio of vCPUs to memory, ideal for batch processing, scientific computing, and high-performance web servers.

D.reason: Storage-optimized instances with high-speed NVMe SSDs, designed for workloads that need very high I/O throughput.

questions.Tips: Match the letter to the function: C = Compute, R = RAM/Memory, T = burstable (cheap), I = I/O storage. 'Compute-intensive' maps directly to the C-series.

options.A: A physical data center security rule
options.B: A JSON document that defines permissions granted to an IAM entity
options.C: A billing rule that controls AWS spending limits

A.description: A physical data center security rule
A.reason: Physical security at AWS data centers is managed by AWS infrastructure teams, not IAM.

B.description: A JSON document that defines permissions granted to an IAM entity
B.reason: IAM Policies are structured JSON documents that specify which actions are allowed or denied on which resources and under what conditions.

C.description: A billing rule that controls AWS spending limits
C.reason: Billing controls are managed through AWS Budgets and Cost Management, not IAM.

D.reason: Network-level controls are handled by Security Groups and Network ACLs, not IAM.

questions.Tips: IAM policies are the permission layer, not a network or billing tool. Eliminate the three non-IAM concepts (firewall, billing, physical security). The remaining JSON document definition is the answer.

questions.questionText: Which EC2 feature allows you to run a script automatically when an instance first launches?

A.reason: A reusable configuration template for launching EC2 instances (specifying AMI, instance type, security groups, etc.). It does not execute scripts by itself, though it can include a User Data field.

B.reason: A script (bash, PowerShell, etc.) that runs automatically as root/administrator the first time an EC2 instance boots. Commonly used to install software and configure the environment.

C.reason: A service accessible from within the instance at `169.254.169.254` that provides information about the instance itself (instance ID, region, IAM role, etc.). It does not run scripts.

D.reason: A point-in-time backup of an EBS volume. It has nothing to do with launching or configuring instances.

questions.Tips: User Data runs scripts at first boot. Metadata *reads* info. Snapshots *back up* disks. Launch Templates define config but don't *run* scripts themselves. Only User Data executes code.

questions.questionText: What is the default behavior for a newly created IAM User with no policies attached?

options.A: The user has full administrator access
options.B: The user has read-only access to all services
options.C: The user has no permissions (implicit deny)
options.D: The user inherits the root account's permissions

A.description: The user has full administrator access
A.reason: Administrator access is a privilege that must be explicitly granted, typically via the `AdministratorAccess` managed policy.

B.description: The user has read-only access to all services
B.reason: Read-only access is also an explicit grant, not a default.

C.description: The user has no permissions (implicit deny)
C.reason: AWS IAM uses a default-deny model. Unless a policy explicitly allows an action, it is denied. A user with no attached policies cannot perform any actions.

D.description: The user inherits the root account's permissions
D.reason: IAM Users are completely separate identities from the root account. There is no automatic inheritance of root permissions.

questions.Tips: AWS is default-deny. A new user with no policies is like a blank slate — no permissions at all. Eliminate any option suggesting inherited or automatic access.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a valid EC2 instance state?

A.reason: Not a valid EC2 instance state. Archiving is a concept used in Amazon S3 Glacier for storage, not for EC2 instances.

B.reason: Not a valid EC2 state. Suspension is used in other contexts (e.g., AWS account suspension), but not for individual EC2 instances.

C.reason: While EC2 Hibernation is a feature, "Hibernating" is a transitional action, not a named resting state. The instance enters a stopped state after hibernation completes.

D.reason: A fully valid EC2 instance state. A stopped instance is not running, is not billed for compute, but retains its EBS volumes and configuration. It can be restarted at any time.

questions.Tips: Know the 5 EC2 states: Pending → Running → Stopping → Stopped → Terminated. 'Archived', 'Suspended', and 'Hibernating' are not named EC2 states. Stopped is the valid resting state.
